proposition_text: Greater participation by government-hired PMSCs in reported events is associated with shorter civil wars, plausibly because event participation proxies the resources a government is willing and able to commit to the conflict — independent of whether multiple PMSCs are competing for the contract.
theoretical_basis: Event participation as a proxy for government resource commitment to the conflict, distinct from market-competition mechanisms emphasized in Akcinaroglu & Radziszewski (2013).
scope_conditions: African civil wars 1990–2008; results do not generalize automatically to rebel-hired PMSC events (smaller sample, different incentive structure) or to non-African conflicts.

proposition_text: Akcinaroglu & Radziszewski (2013)'s claim that competition among government-hired PMSCs shortens civil wars is not robust to controlling for the count of events those PMSCs participated in: once event participation is in the model, competition loses statistical significance, suggesting competition is not the operative mechanism.
theoretical_basis: Methodological reanalysis: a confounder (event participation) that correlates with both PMSC competition and conflict duration explains away the competition-duration relationship.
scope_conditions: African civil wars 1990–2008; A&R 2013 replication panel; Cox proportional-hazards specification. PSED authors are explicit that the result does not rule out a role for competition but shifts the burden of proof.
